bibliographic_data.title: What's the Gist? Privacy-Preserving Aggregation of User Profiles
bibliographic_data.abstract: Over the past few years, online service providers have started gathering increasing amounts of personal information to build user profiles and monetize them with advertisers and data brokers. Users have little control over what information is processed and are often left with an all-or-nothing decision between receiving free services or refusing to be profiled. This paper explores an alternative approach where users only disclose an aggregate model – the “gist” – of their data. We aim to preserve data utility and simultaneously provide user privacy. We show that this approach can be efficiently supported by letting users contribute encrypted and differentially-private data to an aggregator. The aggregator combines encrypted contributions and can only extract an aggregate model of the underlying data. We evaluate our framework on a dataset of 100,000 U.S. users obtained from the U.S. Census Bureau and show that (i) it provides accurate aggregates with as little as 100 users, (ii) it generates revenue for both users and data brokers, and (iii) its overhead is appreciably low.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a novel approach to privacy-preserving aggregation of user data where users contribute encrypted and differentially-private data to an aggregator, which can only extract an aggregate model of the underlying data.
core_content.key_methodology: Users combine their data into an aggregate model – the “gist” – and contribute this to an aggregator. The aggregator combines these contributions to generate statistical models without revealing individual user information.
outcomes: Accurate aggregates with as little as 100 users
Revenue generation for both users and data brokers
Low computational overhead on user devices
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the challenge of privacy-preserving aggregation of user data. It proposes a method where users contribute encrypted and differentially-private data to an aggregator, which can only extract aggregate models without revealing individual information. The authors evaluate this framework using a dataset of 100,000 U.S. users from the Census Bureau, demonstrating that it provides accurate aggregates with as few as 100 participants, generates revenue for both parties, and has low computational overhead.
